title: Job 9
subtitle: Job’s Reply: How Can a Mortal Contend with God?

heb.paragraph: "How can mere mortals prove their righteousness (ṣādaq) before God?" (v.2). The legal terminology: ṣādaq means to win one’s case, to be vindicated in court. Job accepts Eliphaz’s premise (4:17) but draws a different conclusion: the problem is not guilt but powerlessness. Even an innocent person cannot get a fair hearing against God.

refs.note: "He set the earth on its foundations" — the same creation power, but in the Psalm it’s praise; in Job it’s the problem.
"He who forms the mountains, creates the wind" — God’s creative power as evidence of sovereignty.

notes.note: "How can mere mortals prove their righteousness before God? Though they wished to dispute with him, they could not answer him one time out of a thousand." The courtroom problem: God is both opponent and judge.

"He moves mountains without their knowing... he shakes the earth from its place... he speaks to the sun and it does not shine." A doxology of terror: God’s power is so absolute that contesting him is futile.

"He performs wonders that cannot be fathomed, miracles that cannot be counted." Job quotes Eliphaz (5:9) — the same words, opposite conclusion. Eliphaz meant: trust God’s goodness. Job means: God’s power makes justice impossible.
"When he passes me, I cannot see him; when he moves by, I cannot perceive him." God is invisible and irresistible. You cannot see the judge, and you cannot stop the judgment.
Calvin: Job’s question is not about moral guilt but procedural justice. How can a creature contest with the Creator? The categories are incommensurate.
Calvin: Job’s praise of God’s power is sincere. His problem is not that God is powerful but that power without accountability is terrifying for the sufferer.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes ṣādaq is a legal term — "be declared righteous in court." Job frames the entire question as a lawsuit he cannot win.
The NET: "treads on the waves of the sea" — God walks on the sea as on pavement. The image anticipates Jesus in Matt 14:25.

clines.source: David J.A. Clines, Job, WBC (3 vols., 1989–2011) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Clines: the legal framing is critical. Job does not say "I am guilty." He says: "Even if I’m innocent, I can’t win." The courtroom is rigged by the power differential.
Clines: Job’s doxology is genuine but terrifying. He praises the same God he accuses. The power he describes is the power he cannot contest.

habel.source: Norman C. Habel, The Book of Job, OTL (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Habel: Job confronts the fundamental problem of his legal case. He has a complaint, but the defendant (God) is also the judge. No human court can adjudicate between a mortal and the Almighty.

hist.context: Job agrees with Eliphaz that no mortal can be righteous before God — but not because all mortals are guilty. Rather, because God is too powerful to be challenged. The power differential makes fair adjudication impossible. God moves mountains, shakes the earth, seals up the stars, walks on the waves (vv.5–10). Against such power, what court can function?

sections.header: Verses 13–24 — "Even If I Were Innocent, I Could Not Answer"

refs.note: "The same destiny overtakes all" — the same collapse of the moral distinction between righteous and wicked.
"For your sake we face death all day long" — the righteous suffering without explanation.

notes.note: "How then can I dispute with him? Even if I were innocent, I could not answer him; I could only plead with my Judge for mercy." Job acknowledges the power gap: even innocence cannot force a hearing.
"Even if I were innocent, my mouth would condemn me... He destroys both the blameless and the wicked." This is the most dangerous verse in the dialogue: Job accuses God of moral indifference.

"When a scourge brings sudden death, he mocks at the despair of the innocent. When a land falls into the hands of the wicked, he blindfolds its judges." Job accuses God of laughing at innocent suffering and corrupting justice.
Calvin: Job’s words go too far here. "He destroys both the blameless and the wicked" attributes moral indifference to God. Calvin: this is grief speaking beyond its warrant, not considered theology.
Calvin: "if it is not he, then who?" is the logical conclusion of strict monotheism confronted with injustice. Job is more honest than the friends, even when he is wrong.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: even Job’s attempt to declare innocence would backfire — his own words would be twisted against him in the divine court.
The NET flags this as one of Job’s most theologically charged statements — God mocking the innocent is an accusation of divine sadism.

clines.source: David J.A. Clines, Job, WBC (3 vols., 1989–2011) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Clines: "He destroys both the blameless and the wicked" — Job collapses the moral categories the friends depend on. If God treats everyone the same, retribution theology is meaningless.
Clines: "If it is not he, then who is it?" Job’s rhetorical question. If God is sovereign and injustice exists, God is responsible. There is no one else to blame.

habel.source: Norman C. Habel, The Book of Job, OTL (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Habel: Job’s case reaches its most aggressive point. He accuses the judge of bias, mockery, and complicity with injustice. This is contempt of court — and it is the only honest response to innocent suffering within a monotheistic framework.

hist.context: Job descends into despair about justice. Even if he summoned God and God responded, Job doesn’t believe God would listen (v.16). Even if he were innocent, his own mouth would condemn him (v.20). Whether innocent or guilty, God destroys (v.22). "When a scourge brings sudden death, he mocks at the calamity of the innocent" (v.23) — Job’s most daring accusation so far.

sections.header: Verses 25–35 — "There Is No Arbiter Between Us"

heb.paragraph: "If only there were someone to mediate (mōkîyaḥ) between us" (v.33). The first cry for a mediator — someone who could lay his hand on both parties and adjudicate fairly. Job needs a third party because God is both his opponent and his judge. This longing will escalate: witness in heaven (16:19), redeemer who lives (19:25).

refs.note: "There is one mediator between God and mankind, the man Christ Jesus" — the answer to Job’s cry, across the canon.
"Christ is the mediator of a new covenant" — the arbiter Job longed for.

notes.note: "My days are swifter than a runner." Life races past. Even if Job could cleanse himself, the window is closing.
"Even if I washed myself with soap... you would plunge me into a slime pit." Self-purification is useless because God would re-contaminate him. The court is unfair.
"If only there were someone to mediate between us." The mōkîyaḥ — the umpire, the arbiter. Job’s longing for a third party who could bridge the gap between creature and Creator.
"Someone to remove God’s rod from me, so that his terror would frighten me no more." Job wants the fear removed so he can speak freely. He cannot make his case while God’s power silences him.
Calvin: the cry for a mediator anticipates Christ. Job did not know what he was asking for, but the Holy Spirit was pointing forward to the one who would lay his hand on both God and man.
Calvin: Job wants to speak without terror. The gospel provides exactly this: "Let us approach God’s throne of grace with confidence" (Heb 4:16) — because the mediator exists.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes mōkîyaḥ is a legal term for an arbiter who adjudicates between two parties. Job wants someone who has authority over both.
The NET: "as things now stand with me" — literally "for I am not so with myself." Job is not himself; fear has distorted his ability to speak.

clines.source: David J.A. Clines, Job, WBC (3 vols., 1989–2011) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Clines: the mōkîyaḥ is the book’s first step toward the redeemer of 19:25. Job’s theology develops: from "I need an umpire" to "I know my redeemer lives." The longing deepens through the dialogue.
Clines: "I would speak up without fear of him, but as things now stand with me, I cannot." Job’s inability to speak freely is the core procedural injustice. He has a case but cannot make it.

habel.source: Norman C. Habel, The Book of Job, OTL (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Habel: the mōkîyaḥ request is the pivotal legal moment. Job recognises that without a neutral arbiter, his case is hopeless. The plaintiff cannot get a fair hearing from a judge who is also the accused.

hist.context: Job’s legal frustration reaches its climax. His days are swift (v.25), he cannot cleanse himself (v.30–31), and there is no umpire to level the playing field (v.33). The mediator cry is the theological seed that grows through the dialogue: Job needs someone between himself and God. The NT will identify this mediator as Christ (1 Tim 2:5).

ppl.text: Job agrees with Eliphaz that no mortal can be righteous before God — but not because all mortals are guilty…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV“Indeed, I know that this is true. But how can mere mortals prove their innocence before God?ESV“Truly I know that it is so: But how can a man be in the right before God?NIVHe is the Maker of the Bear and Orion, the Pleiades and the constellations of the south.ESVwho made the Bear and Orion, the Pleiades and the chambers of the south;

rows.label: Power — 9:1–12
rows.text: God moves mountains and walks on waves. Too powerful to contest.

rows.label: Indifference — 9:13–24
rows.text: "He destroys both blameless and wicked." God mocks the innocent.

hebtext.note: "How can mere mortals prove their righteousness (ṣādaq) before God?" (v.2). The legal terminology: ṣādaq means to win one’s case, to be vindicated in court. Job accepts Eliphaz’s premise (4:17) but draws a different conclusion: the problem is not guilt but powerlessness. Even an innocent person cannot get a fair hearing against God.

"If only there were someone to mediate (mōkîyaḥ) between us" (v.33). The first cry for a mediator — someone who could lay his hand on both parties and adjudicate fairly. Job needs a third party because God is both his opponent and his judge. This longing will escalate: witness in heaven (16:19), redeemer who lives (19:25).

thread.text: "He set the earth on its foundations" — the same creation power, but in the Psalm it’s praise; in Job it’s the problem.
"He who forms the mountains, creates the wind" — God’s creative power as evidence of sovereignty.
"The same destiny overtakes all" — the same collapse of the moral distinction between righteous and wicked.
"For your sake we face death all day long" — the righteous suffering without explanation.
"There is one mediator between God and mankind, the man Christ Jesus" — the answer to Job’s cry, across the canon.
"Christ is the mediator of a new covenant" — the arbiter Job longed for.

themes.note: Sovereignty and Justice both peak: God’s unchecked power makes justice impossible. Prophecy rises: the mediator cry points toward Christ (1 Tim 2:5).
